item.title: CVE-2026-3300 Exploited to Take Over Everest Forms Pro WordPress Sites
item.description: This digest window is dominated by active exploitation of a critical WordPress plugin vulnerability affecting Everest Forms Pro. The reporting highlights a familiar high-impact web compromise pattern: vulnerable plugin functionality enabling code execution, followed by attacker-created...
item.content:encoded: This digest window is dominated by active exploitation of a critical WordPress plugin vulnerability affecting Everest Forms Pro. The reporting highlights a familiar high-impact web compromise pattern: vulnerable plugin functionality enabling code execution, followed by attacker-created administrator accounts for persistent site control. Attackers are actively exploiting CVE-2026-3300, a critical vulnerability in Everest Forms Pro versions 1.9.12 and earlier, to take over WordPress websites. The flaw resides in the plugin’s Complex Calculation feature, where submitted form values are inserted into a PHP code string and executed through PHP’s eval() function, creating a path to arbitrary code execution in vulnerable installations. According to Wordfence telemetry cited in the report, exploitation has been used to create rogue WordPress administrator accounts. One observed malicious account username was “diksimarina,” indicating that attackers are leveraging the vulnerability not merely for proof-of-concept activity but for direct administrative persistence on compromised sites. The vulnerability was submitted to Wordfence by researcher h0xilo in February, and the Everest Forms developer released a patch on March 18. Wordfence reported that active exploitation began on April 13 and that its firewall has blocked more than 29,300 exploitation attempts, underscoring broad scanning and exploitation activity against exposed WordPress deployments running affected plugin versions.

item.title: Miasma Worm Hits Microsoft Repositories and DocuSign Homoglyph Phishing Bypasses CDR
item.description: This coverage window highlights two trust-abuse incidents affecting software development and enterprise messaging workflows. Both reports center on adversaries exploiting implicitly trusted channels: a compromised GitHub contributor account used to target AI coding agents, and an email security...
item.content:encoded: This coverage window highlights two trust-abuse incidents affecting software development and enterprise messaging workflows. Both reports center on adversaries exploiting implicitly trusted channels: a compromised GitHub contributor account used to target AI coding agents, and an email security allow-list that permitted delivery of a multi-domain DocuSign-themed phishing message despite failed authentication signals. StepSecurity reported that on June 5, 2026, a malicious commit was pushed to the Azure/durabletask repository using a previously compromised contributor account. The commit added configuration files for Claude Code, Gemini CLI, Cursor, and VS Code that either executed or instructed execution of a credential-harvesting payload when the repository was opened, positioning the attack specifically against AI-assisted coding workflows. The report links this repository injection to the May 19 durabletask PyPI compromise through the same compromised contributor account. GitHub’s automated enforcement disabled 73 repositories across the Azure, microsoft, Azure-Samples, and MicrosoftDocs organizations within a 105-second window. One operational impact was the disabling of Azure/functions-action, which caused GitHub workflows referencing Azure/functions-action@v1 to stop resolving and disrupted CI/CD pipelines for developers. The incident illustrates how a single compromised identity in a large open-source and cloud development ecosystem can cascade across repositories, package infrastructure, and dependent automation. IRONSCALES separately detailed a phishing message impersonating DocuSign using the display name “Vantage via D0cuSign,” substituting a zero for the letter O. The message showed significant domain inconsistency, with yshfoodsupply.com in the From address, hansabusad.com in the Return-Path, vantage.bank in the Reply-To, and candybreak.net in the X-Relaying-Domain header. SPF, DKIM, DMARC, and CompAuth all returned none or fail, but the message was delivered with SCL=-1 after passing through an allow-listed Votiro CDR relay at 44.206.213.130. The phishing email included a Mailchimp redirect call-to-action labeled “Review Document,” an invite.ics calendar attachment, and a document.png attachment served as image/jpeg. Themis scored the message at 66% confidence, identified the recipient as a VIP, and one mailbox was quarantined. Together with the Microsoft repository incident, the report underscores a recurring pattern in which adversaries rely less on novel malware and more on abusing trusted intermediaries, workflow automation, and security exceptions to reach high-value users and systems.

item.title: Miasma Hits Microsoft GitHub as CVE-2026-28318 Enters CISA KEV
item.description: This digest is led by software supply chain risk, actively exploited enterprise vulnerabilities, and emerging evidence of AI accelerating vulnerability discovery. The most operationally significant items are the Miasma worm’s impact across Microsoft GitHub repositories, CISA’s addition of...
item.content:encoded: This digest is led by software supply chain risk, actively exploited enterprise vulnerabilities, and emerging evidence of AI accelerating vulnerability discovery. The most operationally significant items are the Miasma worm’s impact across Microsoft GitHub repositories, CISA’s addition of SolarWinds Serv-U CVE-2026-28318 to the KEV catalog, and large vulnerability disclosures affecting FFmpeg and Chrome. The Miasma self-replicating supply chain campaign reportedly affected 73 Microsoft GitHub repositories across the Azure, Azure-Samples, Microsoft, and MicrosoftDocs organizations. GitHub disabled access to affected repositories, including Azure/azure-functions-host, citing a violation of its terms of service. The campaign included a re-compromise of the durabletask PyPI package, which had previously been infected by TeamPCP to deliver an information stealer on Linux systems. Miasma is assessed to be a variant of the Mini Shai-Hulud worm that TeamPCP publicly released in mid-May 2026, indicating reuse or adaptation of publicly available worming tradecraft. Reported behavior includes pushing malicious code directly to GitHub repositories and wiring execution through developer tooling such as Claude Code, Gemini CLI, Cursor, VS Code, and the npm test script. This positions the campaign at the intersection of repository compromise, package ecosystem abuse, and AI-assisted developer workflow exploitation. CISA added CVE-2026-28318, a high-severity SolarWinds Serv-U vulnerability, to the Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog after evidence of active exploitation. The flaw is an uncontrolled resource consumption issue with a CVSS score of 7.5 that can allow an unauthenticated attacker to crash Serv-U using specially crafted POST requests with Content-Encoding: deflate. SolarWinds fixed the issue in Serv-U version 15.5.4 HF1 and described mitigations including limiting access to known addresses and blocking requests containing "content-encoding." CISA directed Federal Civilian Executive Branch agencies to address the vulnerability by June 19, 2026. Separate vulnerability research highlighted the growing role of autonomous systems in bug discovery. Security startup depthfirst reported that an autonomous AI agent found 21 previously unknown vulnerabilities in FFmpeg during a run costing about $1,000, with some flaws dating back 15 to 20 years, including a stack overflow in service-description-table code from 2003. Google also released Chrome 149 with fixes for 429 security vulnerabilities, including more than 100 rated critical or high severity. The most severe issue, CVE-2026-10881, is an out-of-bounds read and write in the ANGLE graphics engine that can allow a crafted page to escape the browser sandbox and execute code on the host. Research by Include Security and independent researcher Buchodi found that Bright Data’s iOS SDK can turn consumer devices into exit nodes for web-scraping traffic routed through users’ home internet connections. The analysis found that the peer channel carrying scraping jobs had no real authentication and that, on iOS, SDK traffic could bypass a configured VPN. Bright Data says its residential proxy supply includes consent-sourced IPs from an SDK embedded in free apps behind an opt-in screen. The reporting raises questions about whether disclosed consent accurately reflects SDK capabilities, citing a Roku app called Petflix whose opt-in screen described only occasional device and connection use while SDK settings allowed up to 200 GB of traffic per month. The report identified several Bright Data SDK domains associated with the relay function, including proxyjs.brdtnet.com and clientsdk.bright-sdk.com. The findings broaden the residential proxy risk model beyond traditional malware-driven botnets to include app-mediated monetization channels on consumer smart devices.

item.title: Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Manager CVE-2026-20245 Actively Exploited
item.description: During this coverage window, reporting centered on active exploitation of a high-severity Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Manager vulnerability with no patch currently available. The activity is notable because exploitation can lead to root-level command execution on affected management systems and, in...
item.content:encoded: During this coverage window, reporting centered on active exploitation of a high-severity Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Manager vulnerability with no patch currently available. The activity is notable because exploitation can lead to root-level command execution on affected management systems and, in limited observed cases, configuration changes propagated to SD-WAN edge devices. Cisco disclosed that CVE-2026-20245 in Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Manager is being actively exploited. The flaw allows an authenticated local attacker with netadmin privileges to upload a crafted file and execute arbitrary commands as root on an affected system. The exploitation path is constrained by credential and access requirements: Cisco stated that successful exploitation requires valid credentials or prior exploitation of related vulnerabilities, specifically CVE-2026-20182 or CVE-2026-20127. This places CVE-2026-20245 within a broader intrusion chain in which attackers may first obtain access through other flaws or compromised accounts before escalating control over the SD-WAN management plane. Cisco reported limited cases in which exploitation of CVE-2026-20245 resulted in a configuration change being pushed to edge devices. That detail is significant for defenders because SD-WAN Manager occupies a central orchestration role, and unauthorized changes at the management layer can have downstream operational impact across distributed network infrastructure. At the time of reporting, Cisco said there were no patches or mitigations available for CVE-2026-20245. The company linked exposure to the broader vulnerability set affecting SD-WAN software and referenced fixes for CVE-2026-20182 as part of the surrounding risk context. The reported exploitation remains described as limited, but the vulnerability’s location in a network management platform increases its strategic importance. For threat hunters, detection engineers, SOC analysts, and incident responders, the key intelligence value is the combination of active exploitation, root-level execution potential, authenticated access requirements, and observed propagation of configuration changes to managed edge infrastructure.

item.title: Miasma Hits Microsoft GitHub as CVE-2026-3300, Serv-U, and Cisco SD-WAN Face Exploitation
item.description: This 24-hour digest is dominated by active exploitation of enterprise and web-facing software, a large supply chain incident affecting Microsoft GitHub repositories, and continued security pressure around AI-enabled development workflows. The reporting also highlights abuse of trust mechanisms in...
item.content:encoded:
This 24-hour digest is dominated by active exploitation of enterprise and web-facing software, a large supply chain incident affecting Microsoft GitHub repositories, and continued security pressure around AI-enabled development workflows. The reporting also highlights abuse of trust mechanisms in email, consumer devices, and AI tools, where legitimate infrastructure or features can become conduits for credential theft, data exposure, or traffic relay. Attackers are actively exploiting CVE-2026-3300 in Everest Forms Pro for WordPress, affecting versions 1.9.12 and earlier. The flaw resides in the plugin’s Complex Calculation feature, where submitted form values are inserted into PHP code and executed via eval(), enabling site takeover. Wordfence telemetry showed attackers creating rogue administrator accounts, including one using the username “diksimarina,” and reported more than 29,300 blocked exploitation attempts since exploitation began on April 13. CISA added CVE-2026-28318 in SolarWinds Serv-U to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog following evidence of active exploitation. The high-severity uncontrolled resource consumption flaw can allow unauthenticated crashes through specially crafted POST requests using Content-Encoding: deflate, causing a denial-of-service condition. SolarWinds fixed the issue in Serv-U 15.5.4 HF1, and CISA set a June 19, 2026 remediation deadline for Federal Civilian Executive Branch agencies. Cisco also warned of active exploitation of CVE-2026-20245 in Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Manager. The vulnerability allows an authenticated local attacker with netadmin privileges to execute arbitrary commands as root by uploading a crafted file, with Cisco observing limited cases where exploitation led to configuration changes pushed to edge devices. Cisco said no patch or mitigation is currently available for CVE-2026-20245, and exploitation requires valid credentials or prior exploitation of related SD-WAN flaws. The Miasma self-replicating supply chain campaign impacted 73 Microsoft GitHub repositories across the Azure, Azure-Samples, Microsoft, and MicrosoftDocs organizations. Reporting from StepSecurity and The Hacker News linked the June 5 repository injections to a previously compromised contributor account also associated with the May 19 durabletask PyPI compromise. GitHub disabled the affected repositories in a 105-second automated enforcement window, including repositories such as Azure/azure-functions-host. The malicious commits added configuration files for Claude Code, Gemini CLI, Cursor, and VS Code that executed or instructed execution of credential-harvesting payloads when repositories were opened. The disabling of Azure/functions-action disrupted GitHub workflows referencing Azure/functions-action@v1, causing CI/CD pipeline failures for developers. Miasma is assessed as a variant of the Mini Shai-Hulud worm publicly released by TeamPCP in mid-May 2026 and has also been observed wiring execution through npm test scripts. OpenAI began rolling out an optional ChatGPT Lockdown Mode for eligible personal accounts and self-serve ChatGPT Business plans. The feature is designed to reduce data exfiltration risk from prompt injection by limiting capabilities that connect to the web or external services, including live browsing, image support, deep research, agent mode, Canvas networking, and file downloads for data analysis. OpenAI also introduced account session management so users can review and terminate active ChatGPT sessions. Separately, security startup depthfirst reported that an autonomous AI agent found 21 previously unknown vulnerabilities in FFmpeg during a run costing about $1,000. Some flaws reportedly dated back 15 to 20 years, including a stack overflow in service-description-table code from 2003. Google also released Chrome 149 with fixes for 429 security vulnerabilities, including CVE-2026-10881, an ANGLE graphics engine out-of-bounds read/write issue that can allow a crafted page to escape the sandbox and run code on the host. IRONSCALES detailed a phishing message using the display name “Vantage via D0cuSign,” replacing the “O” in DocuSign with a zero. The campaign used four unrelated domains across sender, return-path, reply-to, and relaying headers, while SPF, DKIM, DMARC, and CompAuth returned none or fail. Despite those failures, the message was delivered with SCL=-1 after passing through an allow-listed Votiro CDR relay, and it included a Mailchimp redirect, calendar invite, and mismatched image attachment. Research from Include Security and Buchodi found that Bright Data’s SDK can turn consumer devices, including smart TVs and mobile devices, into residential proxy exit nodes for web-scraping traffic. The researchers reported weak authentication on the peer channel carrying scraping jobs and said the SDK’s traffic on iOS can bypass a configured VPN. In one Roku app, Petflix, the opt-in screen described occasional use while SDK settings allowed up to 200 GB of traffic per month.

Miasma Worm Hits Microsoft Again: Azure Functions Action and 72 Other Repositories Disabled After Supply Chain Attack Targeting AI Coding Agents — Must Read

The Miasma worm campaign hit Microsoft’s Azure GitHub repositories by planting malicious editor and AI-coding-agent configuration files that executed a credential-stealing payload when developers opened affected repos. GitHub disabled 73 Microsoft-related repositories, including Azure/functions-action, disrupting CI/CD pipelines and highlighting a shift from package-install attacks to “folder-open” supply chain compromise.

  • On June 5, 2026, a malicious commit was pushed to the Azure/durabletask repository using a previously compromised contributor account.
  • The commit added configuration files for Claude Code, Gemini CLI, Cursor, and VS Code that executed or instructed execution of a credential-harvesting payload when the repository was opened.
  • GitHub disabled 73 repositories across the Azure, microsoft, Azure-Samples, and MicrosoftDocs GitHub organizations in a 105-second automated enforcement window.
  • The disabling of Azure/functions-action caused GitHub workflows referencing Azure/functions-action@v1 to stop resolving, breaking CI/CD pipelines for developers.
  • The article links the June 5 repository injection to the May 19 durabletask PyPI compromise through the same compromised contributor account.

Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Manager CVE-2026-20245 Flaw Actively Exploited – No Patch Available — Must Read

Cisco says CVE-2026-20245 in Catalyst SD-WAN Manager is being actively exploited to let authenticated netadmin users execute commands as root, and no patch or mitigation is currently available. Customers should ensure fixes for related auth-bypass flaws are applied and check logs for indicators of compromise, especially on internet-exposed systems.

  • Cisco warned that CVE-2026-20245, a high-severity flaw in Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Manager, is being actively exploited.
  • CVE-2026-20245 allows an authenticated local attacker with netadmin privileges to execute arbitrary commands as root by uploading a crafted file to an affected system.
  • Cisco said exploitation of CVE-2026-20245 requires valid credentials or prior exploitation of CVE-2026-20182 or CVE-2026-20127.
  • Cisco observed limited cases where exploitation of CVE-2026-20245 resulted in a configuration change pushed to edge devices.
  • Cisco said there are currently no patches or mitigations available for CVE-2026-20245 and advised customers to upgrade SD-WAN software to apply fixes for CVE-2026-20182.

Critical Everest Forms Pro flaw exploited to take over WordPress sites — Must Read

Hackers are actively exploiting a critical unauthenticated code execution flaw in Everest Forms Pro versions 1.9.12 and earlier to create rogue WordPress administrator accounts. Site owners should update immediately, check for suspicious admin users such as “diksimarina,” and review logs for compromise.

  • Hackers are actively exploiting CVE-2026-3300, a critical vulnerability in Everest Forms Pro versions 1.9.12 and earlier, to take complete control of WordPress websites.
  • The vulnerability exists in the plugin’s Complex Calculation feature, where submitted form values are inserted into a PHP code string and executed with PHP’s eval() function.
  • Wordfence telemetry shows attackers are exploiting the flaw to create rogue administrator accounts, including one with the username “diksimarina.”
  • The Everest Forms developer released a patch for CVE-2026-3300 on March 18 after researcher h0xilo submitted the vulnerability through Wordfence in February.
  • Wordfence reported that active exploitation began on April 13 and that its firewall has blocked more than 29,300 exploitation attempts.

CISA Adds Actively Exploited SolarWinds Serv-U DoS Flaw to KEV Catalog — Must Read

CISA added CVE-2026-28318, an actively exploited SolarWinds Serv-U denial-of-service flaw, to its KEV catalog and ordered federal agencies to remediate it by June 19, 2026. SolarWinds has patched the issue in Serv-U 15.5.4 HF1 and recommends limiting access or blocking requests with Content-Encoding.

  • CISA added CVE-2026-28318, a high-severity flaw affecting SolarWinds Serv-U, to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog due to evidence of active exploitation.
  • CVE-2026-28318 has a CVSS score of 7.5 and is an uncontrolled resource consumption vulnerability that can cause a denial-of-service condition.
  • SolarWinds said Serv-U can be crashed without authentication by specially crafted POST requests using Content-Encoding: deflate.
  • SolarWinds fixed the issue in Serv-U version 15.5.4 HF1 and advised limiting access to known addresses and blocking requests containing "content-encoding" as mitigations.
  • CISA ordered Federal Civilian Executive Branch agencies to address the flaw by June 19, 2026.

AI Agent Uncovers 21 Zero-Days in FFmpeg; Chrome Patches Record 429 Bugs — Must Read

AI-driven vulnerability discovery is accelerating sharply, with an autonomous agent finding 21 zero-days in FFmpeg while Chrome patched a record 429 bugs amid rising AI-generated report volume. The key challenge is no longer just finding flaws, but triaging, fixing, and deploying patches fast enough across widely embedded software.

  • Security startup depthfirst reported 21 previously unknown vulnerabilities in FFmpeg that were found by an autonomous AI agent, with the run costing about $1,000.
  • Several of the FFmpeg bugs had existed for 15 to 20 years, including a stack overflow in service-description-table code dating to 2003.
  • Google released Chrome 149 with fixes for 429 security vulnerabilities, including more than 100 critical or high-severity issues.
  • The most severe Chrome flaw, CVE-2026-10881, is an out-of-bounds read and write in the ANGLE graphics engine that can let a crafted page escape the sandbox and run code on the host.
  • The article says organizations should update FFmpeg from upstream or distribution security releases, patch embedded FFmpeg copies, and update Chrome to version 149.0.7827.53 on Linux or 149.0.7827.53/54 on Windows and macOS.

Free Apps Are Quietly Turning Smart TVs Into Web-Scraping Proxies for AI — Should Read

Bright Data’s SDK, embedded in some free apps including smart TV apps, can turn consumer devices into residential proxy exit nodes for web scraping traffic used by AI data businesses. The key risk is that users’ home IP addresses and bandwidth may be used for third-party scraping under consent screens that researchers say do not clearly reflect the scale or behavior of the traffic.

  • Include Security and independent researcher Buchodi reverse-engineered Bright Data’s iOS SDK and found it can turn consumer devices into exit nodes that relay web-scraping traffic through users’ home internet connections.
  • The research found that the peer channel carrying scraping jobs has no real authentication, and on iOS the SDK’s traffic can bypass a configured VPN.
  • Bright Data says its residential proxy supply includes consent-sourced IPs from an SDK embedded in free apps behind an opt-in screen, but the article says the screen may not match what the SDK actually allows.
  • In one Roku app called Petflix, the opt-in screen said the device and connection would be used “occasionally,” while SDK settings allowed up to 200 GB of traffic per month.
  • The article says users can block several Bright Data SDK domains, including proxyjs.brdtnet.com and clientsdk.bright-sdk.com, with router-level tools such as Pi-hole or NextDNS to stop devices from acting as relays.

New ChatGPT Lockdown Mode Limits Tools That Could Enable Data Exfiltration — Should Read

OpenAI is rolling out an optional ChatGPT Lockdown Mode that reduces prompt-injection data exfiltration risk by disabling or limiting web-connected features like live browsing, image retrieval, deep research, agent mode, Canvas networking, and file downloads. The feature is aimed at users handling sensitive data, but OpenAI warns it does not eliminate all prompt-injection risks.

  • OpenAI has begun rolling out an optional Lockdown Mode for eligible ChatGPT personal accounts and self-serve ChatGPT Business plans to reduce the risk of data exfiltration from prompt injection attacks.
  • Lockdown Mode limits many tools and capabilities that can connect to the web or external services, with the goal of reducing outbound network requests that could transmit sensitive data to attacker-controlled infrastructure.
  • The feature disables or limits live web browsing, image support, deep research, agent mode, Canvas networking, and file downloads for data analysis.
  • OpenAI said Lockdown Mode is not intended for everyone and cannot be used at the same time as Developer Mode because enabling one disables the other.
  • OpenAI also launched an account management feature that lets users review active ChatGPT sessions and log out of individual or all sessions if unauthorized account activity is suspected.

Four Domains, One Email: The DocuSign Homoglyph That Rode a CDR Allow-List — May Read

A phishing email impersonating DocuSign/Vantage used mismatched domains, a Mailchimp redirect, attachments, and a D0cuSign homoglyph, but was delivered because an allow-listed Votiro CDR relay overrode failed SPF, DKIM, DMARC, and CompAuth checks. The key lesson is that trusted relay allow-lists can turn total authentication failure into inbox delivery unless messages still receive behavioral and structural analysis.

  • The phishing email used the display name "Vantage via D0cuSign," replacing the letter O in DocuSign with a zero.
  • The message used four unrelated domains across the sender, return-path, reply-to, and X-Relaying-Domain headers: yshfoodsupply.com, hansabusad.com, vantage.bank, and candybreak.net.
  • SPF, DKIM, DMARC, and CompAuth all returned none or fail, but the message was delivered with SCL=-1 because it passed through an allow-listed Votiro CDR relay at 44.206.213.130.
  • The email included a Mailchimp redirect labeled "Review Document," an invite.ics calendar attachment, and a document.png attachment served with a JPEG content type.
  • Themis scored the message at 66% confidence, flagged the recipient as a VIP, and one mailbox was quarantined.

item.title: Polyfill, npm Worms, Salesforce Exposure, and CVE-2026-28318 Exploitation
item.description: This coverage window was dominated by reports of trusted infrastructure abuse across web dependencies, developer ecosystems, cloud identity flows, and SaaS platforms. Several items describe attackers using legitimate services, valid credentials, or misconfigurations to create convincing phishing...
item.content:encoded: This coverage window was dominated by reports of trusted infrastructure abuse across web dependencies, developer ecosystems, cloud identity flows, and SaaS platforms. Several items describe attackers using legitimate services, valid credentials, or misconfigurations to create convincing phishing paths, steal data, or maintain persistence while blending into expected operational patterns. Toshiba and Muji warned visitors after suspicious sign-in prompts appeared on their websites via scripts loaded from polyfill.io. Security researcher Pasquale Pillitteri reported that polyfill.io became active again in late May 2026 and began returning HTTP 401 authentication requests, causing browser login prompts; Japanese media also reported impacts at Zojirushi, FiNC Technologies, Ishiyaku Publishers, and Hobonichi. Multiple reports described continued pressure on developer ecosystems. In one supply chain scenario, TeamPCP exploited a misconfigured GitHub Actions pullrequesttarget workflow in Aqua Security’s Trivy repository, obtained an aqua-bot PAT, and force-pushed release tags in aquasecurity/trivy-action to malicious commits that harvested secrets before running the legitimate scanner. Separate npm campaigns distributed the Rust-based IronWorm stealer and a new Miasma worm variant through poisoned packages, compromised npm accounts, preinstall execution, and a Phantom Gyp technique using binding.gyp. Censys highlighted dangling DNS records as another form of trusted infrastructure exposure. Stale CNAME, NS, and third-party service records can allow attackers to claim abandoned resources and operate phishing pages, malware delivery, fake login portals, redirects, or defacement content under an organization’s legitimate subdomain. Mitiga described an AWS CLI phishing method abusing the aws login --remote flow, where attackers generate a legitimate cross-device authentication URL, lure victims through real AWS sign-in pages, and complete the attacker’s CLI login using the verification code. The proof-of-concept used AWS-hosted infrastructure including S3, DynamoDB, Lambda, API Gateway, EC2, and SSM, placing much of the phishing infrastructure on amazonaws.com domains. A separate Mitiga report attributed Salesforce data exposure to ShinyHunters’ abuse of misconfigured Experience Cloud Guest User Profile permissions rather than a compromise of Salesforce itself. The activity reportedly involved a modified version of Mandiant’s AuraInspector tool to scan Salesforce sites and extract data through the /s/sfsites/aura endpoint, with ShinyHunters claiming access to data from 300 to 400 organizations. Mitiga also published broader analysis on cloud detection and alerting models. The articles argued that attacks increasingly span Okta, Salesforce, AWS, Kubernetes, S3, workloads, and data services as a single attack path, while static correlation can miss credential misuse and living-off-the-land behavior when attackers vary timing, sequence, or methodology. CISA added CVE-2026-28318, a high-severity SolarWinds Serv-U denial-of-service flaw, to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities Catalog after warning of active exploitation. SolarWinds said the issue is caused by uncontrolled resource consumption triggered by crafted unauthenticated POST requests using Content-Encoding: deflate, and released Serv-U 15.5.4 Hotfix 1. BleepingComputer reported on Volexity findings that Chinese espionage group UNC5221, also tracked as VerdantBamboo, used Brickstorm and newly documented malware named Plenet and AgentPSD to access Microsoft 365 environments and maintain access. Volexity found the actor had been present in one victim network for at least 18 months, had compromised the victim’s managed services provider, and later re-entered using stolen credentials to configure SSL VPN access and deploy malware to a Synology NAS device. Netskope summarized a June 2, 2026 Executive Order directing U.S. federal agencies to accelerate AI adoption while strengthening cybersecurity and national security protections. The order calls for Binding Operational Directives through CISA and OMB consultation, an AI cybersecurity clearinghouse, and classified benchmarking of advanced cyber capabilities in AI models. Health-ISAC published its Health Care and Social Assistance Threat Landscape Report on June 5, covering January 1 through March 31, 2026. Produced by ReliaQuest, the report focuses on ransomware and data-leak activity, vulnerability exploitation, attacker targeting, top techniques, data risk protection, and countries targeted in the health care and social assistance sector.

item.title: Asin Android Spyware, Exposed Tank Gauges, and AI Browser Identity Risks
item.description: This digest highlights a mix of enterprise identity risk, critical infrastructure exposure, mobile surveillance activity, and dark web enforcement developments. Across the reporting, a common theme is the expansion of attack surfaces into browsers, SaaS-connected AI agents, internet-exposed...
item.content:encoded: This digest highlights a mix of enterprise identity risk, critical infrastructure exposure, mobile surveillance activity, and dark web enforcement developments. Across the reporting, a common theme is the expansion of attack surfaces into browsers, SaaS-connected AI agents, internet-exposed operational systems, and mobile apps used by targeted communities. Mitiga framed emerging AI PCs as a new enterprise identity risk, arguing that locally running agents with SaaS integrations can hold live credentials and act across business applications. The article focused on Nvidia and Microsoft’s RTX Spark ecosystem, which Mitiga says will support a 120-billion-parameter AI agent on new Windows PCs from major vendors including Dell, HP, ASUS, Lenovo, Microsoft Surface, and MSI. The risk described is not simply local endpoint compromise, but inherited authority across connected applications. Mitiga said a compromised AI PC could allow an attacker to leverage the agent’s permissions across services such as Salesforce, SharePoint, and Workday, while noting that Nvidia’s OpenShell can define agent boundaries but does not replace detection, correlation, audit trails, or incident response capabilities. A separate browser-focused analysis tied the 2026 Verizon DBIR to the growth of shadow AI and credential abuse. It reported that shadow AI became the third most common non-malicious insider action in DLP datasets, representing a fourfold increase year over year, while 39% of breaches involved credential abuse. Keep Aware telemetry cited in the article found widespread personal-account use of AI services on corporate devices and described browser-based credential theft as its leading observed browser attack category. U.S. government agencies and partners issued a joint advisory on internet-exposed automatic tank gauge systems used in fuel and critical infrastructure environments. Shadowserver reported 1,061 exposed automatic tank gauge system IPs on June 5, 2026, with 909 located in the United States. The advisory described active exploitation of weaknesses including hardcoded credentials, authentication bypasses, SQL injection, operating system command execution flaws, and privilege escalation vulnerabilities. According to the reporting, attackers have used these issues to modify compromised systems through command execution, underscoring continued exposure of operational technology components to internet-based access. ESET reported a new Android spyware family codenamed Asin targeting Arabic-speaking users through fake utility, news, PDF reader, and war map applications. The activity was first detected in early 2025 and used websites imitating government news, document tools, and conflict-related information sources. Identified distribution infrastructure included govlens.net, pdf-reader.help, live-war-map.com, c-pdf.net, and syriadefensemap.com. ESET also observed promotion through dedicated Facebook and Telegram accounts, including GovLens on Facebook and liveuamapar on Telegram, and assessed that the lures suggest Arabic-speaking journalists or OSINT practitioners may have been among the intended targets. The cluster remains unattributed. U.S. authorities announced that Darren Hughes of San Jose, California, received a sentence of more than 26 years in federal prison for trafficking fentanyl and methamphetamine through Nemesis Market. Hughes operated a store on the market, offered free methamphetamine samples, and later sold methamphetamine and fentanyl pills to an undercover law enforcement agent in exchange for cryptocurrency. The case followed Hughes’ June 28, 2023 arrest by the Redwood City Police Department after another arranged sale with undercover agents. Detectives found approximately 672 grams of methamphetamine and a loaded 9mm ghost gun with no serial number in his vehicle. Nemesis Market itself was taken down by German and American authorities in March 2024 after growing to more than 150,000 user accounts and 1,100 seller accounts.
